AIDOC prereqs: EXAM>=1.0 (also not_supported in this repo) and RELEASE>=1.0 (supported)

rollup.rationale: AIDOC requires SDK-shipped documentation, metadata, and examples in formats that AI coding agents can ingest (notably: an `AGENTS.md` per the agents.md open format, an `llms.txt` per llms-txt, and per-public-function metadata with examples). The Go server SDK ships none of these today — the repo has no `AGENTS.md`, no `llms.txt`, no `docs/` directory, and no agent-targeted documentation surface. The spec applies-to is `server-sdk,client-sdk`, so it genuinely applies to this SDK; this is a real gap rather than an inapplicability. AIDOC also has prereqs on EXAM (Hello-Apps, also `not-supported` for this repo) and RELEASE (currently `supported`); closing AIDOC presupposes EXAM. A full per-requirement walk across all 28 AIDOC requirements is deferred to a future batch — every one would resolve to `not_supported` against the current repo state, so a brief block accurately conveys the rollup.

rollup.rationale:
ARCO is an explanatory legacy document (id `SDKC-LEGACY-ARCO`) with zero numbered requirements (zero MUST/SHOULD markers; the only headings are descriptive section titles like '1.1 Data store'). Conformance is therefore evaluated by mapping the four conceptual components — data store, data source, evaluator, event processor — to the Go server SDK's actual architecture. All four are present with idiomatic Go interfaces, fully separated:
  • Data store: subsystems/data_store.go (DataStore interface), internal/datastore/in_memory_data_store_impl.go and persistent_data_store_wrapper.go (full Init/Get/GetAll/Upsert/IsInitialized/Close, plus the persistent-store-with-cache wrapper ARCO §1.1.3 describes).
  • Data source: subsystems/data_source.go (DataSource interface), internal/datasource/streaming_data_source.go and polling_data_source.go (with Start/Close lifecycle), plus the data_source_update_sink_impl.go decorator ARCO §1.2.5 describes.
  • Evaluator: lives in the dependency repo go-server-sdk-evaluation (v3.0.1) — separated exactly per ARCO §1.3 ('the evaluator should be testable independently'). The SDK injects flag/segment lookup via a thin DataProvider interface rather than handing the evaluator the raw store.
  • Event processor: lives in the dependency repo go-sdk-events (v3.5.0) and is wired by ldcomponents/send_events.go and the evaluator's event-emitting hooks. The fire-and-forget input-queue + background-flush architecture ARCO §1.4.2 describes is implemented in that dep repo.
Configuration follows the immutable-builder pattern ARCO §3 calls for: ldcomponents.* helper functions return builder structs with narrow setters; the LDClient takes a Config snapshot at construction. No configuration object is mutable post-init.
Rollup is `supported` rather than `partial` because every ARCO-described component is present and structurally aligned. Complexity `moderate` reflects that ARCO's non-normative status means there's no test harness assertion of compliance — judgment is structural, based on file-and-interface presence.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: CHANGELOG.md bottom entry [2.0.0] - 2016-08-08 already references all four components (data store, data source via update processor, evaluator, event processor with summary events).

rollup.rationale: All 4 numbered MUST requirements are implemented in the go-sdk-common dependency repo at `ldattr/ref.go`. The implementation has been stable since v3.0.0 of go-sdk-common (coincident with go-server-sdk v6.0.0, which introduced contexts). ldattr.Ref carries an `err` field set by NewRef when parsing fails (satisfying §1.1.1's 'non-conforming strings MUST NOT be valid' via Ref.Err()), distinguishes the no-leading-slash case as a literal name (§1.2.1, ref.go:79-82), and unescapes ~1→/ and ~0→~ exactly per §1.2.2 and §1.2.3 in the unescapePath helper (ref.go:252-280). The §1.3 'no public API requirements' optional concepts (Depth, Component, Err) are also all present as public methods on Ref. Test coverage in ref_test.go covers all the example evaluations from §1.2.3's table.

requirements.text: Non-conforming strings MUST NOT be considered valid Attribute References.
requirements.rationale: NewRef stores an error in Ref{} for empty input ('' or '/'), trailing slash (extraSlash), invalid escape sequences. Ref.Err() returns this error and any SDK consumer treats the Ref as invalid. unescapePath returns false on '~' followed by anything other than '0' or '1'.

findings.relevance: NewRef returns Ref{err: ErrAttributeEmpty} for '' or '/'.
NewRef returns Ref with err=ErrAttributeExtraSlash for trailing/double-slash paths.
NewRef returns Ref with err=ErrAttributeInvalidEscape when unescapePath fails.
Ref.Err() exposes the validation error to callers.
unescapePath returns ('', false) on any '~' followed by neither '0' nor '1'.

requirements.text: An input string that does not begin with '/' MUST be treated as the literal name of a singular attribute.
requirements.rationale: NewRef explicitly checks for the no-leading-slash case and stores the entire string as singlePathComponent unchanged (no unescaping, no path splitting).

findings.relevance: NewRef short-circuits when referenceString[0] != '/' and returns Ref{singlePathComponent: referenceString, rawPath: referenceString}.
NewLiteralRef does the same for literal-name semantics, used internally where the caller knows the input is not pointer syntax.

requirements.text: In an input string beginning with '/', all instances of the character sequence '~1' MUST be interpreted as '/'.
requirements.rationale: unescapePath maps '~' followed by '1' to '/'. This applies only to the path components after the leading '/' (the leading slash itself is stripped before splitting).

findings.relevance: unescapePath case '1': unescaped = '/'.
Each path component is run through unescapePath after splitting on '/'.

requirements.text: In an input string beginning with '/', all instances of the character sequence '~0' MUST be interpreted as '~'.
requirements.rationale: unescapePath maps '~' followed by '0' to '~'. The order-of-operations note in the spec ('replace ~1 first, then ~0') is satisfied by the single-pass byte scan, which examines each '~' once.

findings.relevance: unescapePath case '0': unescaped = '~'.
Single-pass byte scan ensures order-of-operations correctness without separate find-and-replace passes.

evidence_sources_considered: go-server-sdk/CHANGELOG.md (no mention of auto env attrs across all v7.x releases)

rollup.rationale: The Go server SDK does not auto-collect environment attributes into Contexts. No code in the repo references the spec's concepts (no `EnvironmentAttribute`, no `automaticEnv` / `autoEnv` / `AutoEnv` symbols). The contract-test capability constants in testservice/servicedef do not include the auto-env capability. For a Go server SDK this is a deliberate gap: the spec is primarily aimed at client-side SDKs running in end-user contexts (browser, mobile, embedded) where the execution environment carries useful default attributes. A Go server runs in arbitrary backend environments where there is no canonical 'environment' to harvest from.

rollup.rationale:
BIGSEG is comprehensively implemented across the Go SDK and its two official big segment store integrations (go-server-sdk-redis-redigo and go-server-sdk-dynamodb). All 8 store-interface requirements (§1.2), all 3 hashing requirements (§1.3), all 6 configuration requirements (§1.4), all 10 wrapper requirements (§1.5), all 7 cache requirements (§1.6), all 3 staleness requirements (§1.7), all 9 status-provider requirements (§1.8), all 19 evaluation-integration requirements (§1.9), and §1.10 (SDK-init independence) are satisfied. The §1.11 store data format requirements are satisfied by the official Redis and DynamoDB integrations, which match the spec's key/attribute schemas exactly. One MUST is rated `partial`: 1.2.8 (don't close externally-provided clients) — the Redis integration's Close() unconditionally calls pool.Close() even when the user supplied the pool via PoolInterface(). The DynamoDB integration is compliant; only Redis-redigo has this gap.
Confidence is high because BigSegmentStoreWrapper (subsystems/ldstoreimpl/big_segment_store_wrapper.go) is exposed publicly so the Relay Proxy can reuse it — meaning the wrapper is exercised by both the SDK's own tests and by the Relay Proxy's contract tests. The evaluator integration in the go-server-sdk-evaluation dependency repo includes evaluator_big_segment_test.go, plus go-server-sdk has ldclient_big_segments_test.go for end-to-end coverage.

requirements.text: Big segment store implementations SHOULD use connection pooling.
requirements.rationale: Redis-redigo uses redigo.Pool (configurable via Pool() / PoolInterface() / DialOptions builder methods). DynamoDB uses the AWS SDK v2 client which has built-in connection management.

requirements.text: Big segment store implementations MUST NOT implement their own caching or retry logic.
requirements.rationale: Both Redis and DynamoDB impls are thin: GetMembership directly issues SMEMBERS / GetItem and returns. No internal cache, no retry loop. Caching is the wrapper's job; retries are the application's.

requirements.text: BigSegmentStore MUST provide a Close operation; MAY be omitted in request-scoped runtimes.
requirements.rationale: BigSegmentStore embeds io.Closer at the interface level, so every implementation MUST provide Close(). Both Redis and DynamoDB impls implement it.

findings.relevance: BigSegmentStore embeds io.Closer (compile-time enforcement).

requirements.text: If a store accepts an externally-provided database client, Close MUST NOT close that client; only SDK-created clients may be closed.
requirements.rationale: DynamoDB compliant: Close() only cancels the SDK's internal context (cancelContext()), never disposes the user-provided AWS client. Redis NOT compliant: Close() unconditionally calls pool.Close() even when the user supplied the pool via PoolInterface(), which would close a pool the application may also be using elsewhere.

findings.relevance: Close() only calls cancelContext() — never closes the AWS client (whether user-provided via ClientConfig or SDK-created).
Close() unconditionally calls store.pool.Close(); if the user supplied the pool via PoolInterface(), this closes a pool the user may share for other purposes.
PoolInterface() builder accepts an external pool but the impl doesn't track ownership.

requirements.notes_for_human: Easy fix: store a `userOwnedPool bool` flag in builderOptions; Close() respects it. Worth a small PR to redis-redigo.

rollup.rationale: The Go SDK emits log messages for almost every condition CLM specifies, but the literal wording and (in §1.1.1's case) the log level diverge from the spec template. The spec uses 'log a message of the following form', which is conventionally read as a template requirement (the wording is the form). Under that strict reading, only requirement-by-requirement intent is met — no message text matches verbatim. Under a permissive 'intent only' reading, all requirements except 1.1.3 (closed) and 1.1.5.1 (invalid-payload-debug) would be `full`. We rollup as `partial` to honor the strict template reading; the requirements table below records each delta. Two requirements are genuinely missing implementation: 1.1.3 (clean-close info log) and 1.1.5.1 (invalid-payload debug log). One conditional requirement (1.2.2.1, indefinite blocking) is N/A because zero/negative waitFor in Go means non-blocking, not indefinite.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: The Go SDK has emitted streaming-related log messages since at least v2.0.0; logDataSourceOutageAsErrorAfter (1.1.6.1) was added with the rich data source status mechanism in the v5.0.0 timeframe (CHANGELOG '5.0.0' - 2021-06).

requirements.rationale: The SDK does log when establishing a streaming connection, but at INFO (not DEBUG) and with different wording ('Starting LaunchDarkly streaming connection' / 'Connecting to LaunchDarkly stream'). Intent matched; level and template wording diverge.

findings.relevance: loggers.Info("Starting LaunchDarkly streaming connection") — wrong level (Info vs Debug) and wrong wording.
loggers.Info("Connecting to LaunchDarkly stream") — second connect-attempt message, also Info level.

requirements.notes_for_human: If the spec is interpreted as 'log the appropriate intent at any level' this is `full`; under strict template+level it is `not_supported`. Rated `partial` as a middle ground.

requirements.text: When the SDK successfully establishes a streaming connection it SHOULD log a message of the following form at the `info` level: `LaunchDarkly streaming connection established.`
requirements.rationale: Logged at INFO with wording 'LaunchDarkly streaming is active'. Level matches; wording differs.

findings.relevance: loggers.Info("LaunchDarkly streaming is active") — fires after the first successful event payload.

requirements.text: When the SDK closes a streaming connection for a non-error related reason it SHOULD log a message of the following form at the `info` level: `Closed LaunchDarkly streaming connection.`

requirements.text: When the SDK encounters a permanent error condition with the streaming connection it SHOULD log a message of the following form at the `error` level: `A permanent error has been encountered with the LaunchDarkly streaming connection. It will not retry. Reason: ${error_desc}`

requirements.text: When the SDK receives an invalid payload from the streaming connection it SHOULD log a message of the following form at the `error` level: `LaunchDarkly streaming connection received invalid data in "${type}" message. Re-establishing LaunchDarkly streaming connection.`

requirements.text: When the connection is disconnected for a period of time greater than `logDataSourceOutageAsErrorAfter` time, then it should log a message of the following form at the `error` level: `A streaming connection to LaunchDarkly has not been established within ${logDataSourceOutageAsErrorAfter} after the connection was interrupted.` `The following errors were encountered: ${error_list}` (Conditional — applies because Go SDK supports logDataSourceOutageAsErrorAfter via LoggingConfigurationBuilder.LogDataSourceOutageAsErrorAfter)

The framing wording differs slightly: 'LaunchDarkly data source outage - updates have been unavailable for at least %s with the following errors:' vs the spec's 'A streaming connection to LaunchDarkly has not been established within ${time} after the connection was interrupted.' Level matches.

describeErrors() formats as '${kind} (${count} times)' matching the spec exactly.

requirements.text: When the user-specified initialization timeout is > the max recommended timeout, it SHOULD log a message of the following form at the `warning` level: `Client was configured to block for up to ${given} when initializing. We recommend blocking no longer than ${max}.` The durations MUST include a unit.

Level matches; durations include a unit ('milliseconds') as the spec MUSTs require; wording omits the 'when initializing' phrase but is otherwise spec-aligned. Threshold is hardcoded to 60 seconds (highWaitForDuration in ldclient.go).

requirements.notes_for_human: The MUST clause ('durations MUST include a unit') is satisfied by the 'milliseconds' literal. The 'when initializing' phrasing miss is cosmetic.

requirements.text: Conditional Requirement (applies only if the SDK supports zero/negative initialization timeout meaning 'no timeout' / block indefinitely): When the user-specified initialization timeout is zero or negative, it SHOULD log a message of the following form at the `warning` level: `Client was configured to block indefinitely when initializing. We recommend blocking no longer than ${max}.`
requirements.rationale: Go SDK treats zero/negative `waitFor` as non-blocking (the `if waitFor > 0 && !client.offline` check at ldclient.go:359 falls through to the `go func() { <-closeWhenReady }()` path). The condition (zero/negative means indefinite) does not hold, so the requirement does not apply per the spec's 'If zero/negative means non-blocking, this condition does not apply.'

requirements.notes_for_human: To support indefinite blocking, ldclient would need to interpret zero/negative as 'wait forever'. There's no application demand for this in the Go ecosystem (Go developers use context.WithCancel for indefinite waits at the call site).

rollup.rationale: The Go server SDK does not emit standardized log codes. No `LogCode` / `errorLogCode` / `log_code` symbols exist in the repo. Logging is done via go-sdk-common's `ldlog` package with free-form messages, no per-message numeric/alphanumeric code. CLM (Common Log Messages) classification is `supported` and largely unrelated — CLM standardizes the *text* of common log messages, while CODES proposes a separate numeric code scheme that has not been adopted in this SDK.

rollup.rationale:
CONTEXT v1.0 is an explanatory legacy document (id `SDKC-LEGACY-CONTEXT`) with zero numbered MUST/SHOULD requirements. Conformance is evaluated against the spec's structural sections (§1.1–§1.8). The Go SDK implements every section in the `ldcontext` package of the dependency repo go-sdk-common (v3.4.0):
  • §1.2 Context kinds (single + multi): ldcontext.New, NewWithKind, NewMulti; Multi() and IndividualContextByKind/ByIndex on Context.
  • §1.3 Built-in attributes (kind/key/name/anonymous): Context.Kind(), Key(), Name(), Anonymous(); validation in builder_simple.go.
  • §1.3.4 Attribute value types (bool/number/string/array/object/null with no coercion): represented by ldvalue.Value (typed sum type, no Object→Bool coercion).
  • §1.3.5 Private attributes: PrivateAttributeCount/ByIndex on Context, Builder.PrivateRef setter, supports object-property-level redaction via ldattr.Ref.
  • §1.3.6 Canonicalized key: Context.FullyQualifiedKey() with the spec's exact algorithm (kind:url-encoded-key for individual; alphabetical join for multi; bare key when kind=='user' for legacy compatibility).
  • §1.4 Attribute references: ldattr.Ref (see ATREF entry).
  • §1.5 JSON representation (individual + multi + old-style user): context_marshaling.go, context_unmarshaling.go, context_serialization.go.
  • §1.7 Context type in SDKs: a single Context value type that represents both individual and multi contexts (uniform API surface).
  • §1.8 Validation: TryBuild() returns (Context, error); Build() returns an invalid Context whose Err() is non-nil; deserialization produces an invalid Context for malformed input rather than throwing/panicking.
Comprehensive test coverage in ldcontext/*_test.go. Full harness coverage via sdk-test-harness's context capability (server-side SDK test service in go-server-sdk/testservice/ exercises this).
Rollup is `supported`, complexity `moderate` — the model is large (canonicalized keys, multi-context, private attribute object-property redaction) but the implementation is mature and stable since v6.0.0.

CSPE has two halves: §1.1 specifies the server-side bootstrap data (the SDK adds a `prerequisites` array to each AllFlagsState entry recording the direct prereqs visited during eval), and §1.2 specifies how a client-side SDK consumes that array to emit prereq events.   • The slice ordering is preserved (append in callback fire order, which matches evaluation order, which is spec-mandated).

evidence_sources_considered: go-server-sdk/internal/datasystem/fdv2_datasystem.go (FDv2 type — orchestration layer, runInitializers, runSynchronizers, consumeSynchronizerResults, fallbackCond/recoveryCond, syncFDv1 transition)
go-server-sdk/ldcomponents/data_system_configuration_builder.go (defaults: PollingV2 as initializer, StreamingV2 primary, PollingV2 fallback)

rollup.rationale:
DATASYSTEM v2 (DRAFT) defines the FDv2 orchestration model: Initializers obtain a Basis, Synchronizers keep it fresh (Primary + Fallback with Fallback/Recovery conditions), and a Data System glues them to a Memory Store + optional Persistent Store. The spec also mandates server-directed FDv1 fallback via the `X-LD-FD-Fallback: true` HTTP response header. The Go server SDK ships an FDv2 implementation in internal/datasystem/fdv2_datasystem.go (FDv2 type) backed by internal/datasourcev2/{polling,streaming}_data_source.go.

Status: PARTIAL. The bulk of the spec is satisfied — configurable initializers/synchronizers, Fallback/Recovery via 10-second condition tick, server-directed FDv1 fallback with payload-then-revert ordering, the three-state (REFRESHED/CACHED/DEFAULTS) data-availability model in data_availability.go, no-DS / no-Init / no-Sync configurations all valid, persistent-store outage recovery, and the bulk of the §1.7 orchestration log lines.

The two material gaps are in §1.1.6 + §1.6.1: the Initializer interface (subsystems.DataInitializer.Fetch) returns `(*Basis, error)` rather than the spec's `FetchResult{Basis, fallbackToFDv1, error}`, and PollingProcessor.Fetch (the initializer-mode entry point) does NOT inspect the X-LD-FD-Fallback header. Header inspection only fires in synchronizer mode. Consequently FDv1 directives delivered on the initial polling-init request are silently ignored. This affects §1.1.6 and the initializer leg of §1.6.1; once the polling synchronizer starts, the directive path is fully wired (§1.6.2-1.6.5).
rollup.supported_since_evidence: go-server-sdk CHANGELOG: FDv2 introduced as the default DataSystem in v7.16.0 (2025-04-09); pre-release plumbing landed across v7.13–v7.15.

requirements.text: SDK MUST support configuration of at least two Initializers.
requirements.rationale: DataSystemConfiguration.Initializers is a []DataInitializer slice — any number of initializers may be configured. ldcomponents.DataSystem().Initializers(...) accepts a variadic list.

findings.relevance: type DataSystemConfiguration struct { Initializers []DataInitializer; ... } — slice supports >=2.
DataSystemConfigurationBuilder.Initializers(initializers ...ComponentConfigurer[DataInitializer])

requirements.text: SDK MUST call Initializers in configuration order (FIFO).
requirements.rationale: runInitializers iterates `for _, initializer := range f.initializers` — preserves the slice order.

findings.relevance: for _, initializer := range f.initializers { ... } — strict configuration order.

requirements.text: Init loop MUST terminate on (1) no more initializers, (2) Basis with Selector returned, or (3) FDv1 directive observed.
requirements.rationale: Conditions (1) and (2) are honored: loop falls through when slice exhausts, and `if basis.ChangeSet.Selector().IsDefined() { return }` short-circuits on first initializer with a defined selector. Condition (3) — FDv1 directive observed during init — is NOT honored: PollingProcessor.Fetch does not inspect X-LD-FD-Fallback, so the directive is invisible to runInitializers.

findings.relevance: if basis.ChangeSet.Selector().IsDefined() { ... return } — condition (2).
Loop end → condition (1) (no more initializers).
Fetch() bypasses X-LD-FD-Fallback header inspection — no directive surfacing during initialization.

requirements.notes_for_human: Gap: extend Basis (or wrap in FetchResult) with a fallbackToFDv1 bool and have PollingProcessor.Fetch inspect the response header.

requirements.text: If init exceeds user-specified timeout, SDK MUST asynchronously report timeout (informational; init continues).
requirements.rationale: LDClient.MakeClient passes a closeWhenReady channel to FDv2.Start; the calling goroutine blocks on it with a timeout via WaitFor. If timeout fires, MakeClient returns a timeout error to the user but FDv2 keeps initializing in the background. This matches the spec's 'asynchronous reporting; init continues' contract.

findings.relevance: MakeClient passes a closeWhenReady channel + waits with InitConnectTimeout; on timeout returns a non-fatal error but FDv2 keeps initializing.

requirements.text: If init produces a Basis, SDK MUST pass it to the configured Primary Synchronizer.
requirements.rationale: store.Apply(basis.ChangeSet, basis.Persist) writes the Basis to the Memory Store. The Primary Synchronizer (built from synchronizerBuilders[0]) starts via runSynchronizers and reads the current Selector via store.Selector() when calling Sync(f.store) — so the Basis is implicitly handed off through the shared store, satisfying the spec's intent.

findings.relevance: f.store.Apply(basis.ChangeSet, basis.Persist) — Basis applied before primary sync starts.
sync.Sync(f.store) — synchronizer started against the populated store.

requirements.text: Initializers MUST support a Fetch returning FetchResult{Basis?, fallbackToFDv1 bool, Error?}.
requirements.rationale: DataInitializer.Fetch returns `(*Basis, error)` — Basis carries Persist + Selector + EnvironmentID, but there is NO fallbackToFDv1 boolean on Basis or in the return signature. The spec also wants the directive observable on the initializer leg.

findings.relevance: Fetch(ds DataSelector, ctx context.Context) (*Basis, error) — no fallbackToFDv1 surfaced.
type Basis struct { ChangeSet, Persist, EnvironmentID } — no fallbackToFDv1 field.

requirements.notes_for_human: Trivial source-compat-breaking change: add `RevertToFDv1 bool` to Basis and have PollingProcessor.Fetch populate it from the X-LD-FD-Fallback header.

requirements.text: Initializers MUST indicate whether data should persist via the Basis.persist field.
requirements.rationale: Basis.Persist bool is set by each initializer. PollingProcessor sets Persist=true (LD-authoritative); ldfiledatav2 sets Persist=false (file initializer is non-authoritative).

findings.relevance: type Basis struct { ... Persist bool ... }
PollingProcessor.Fetch returns &subsystems.Basis{ ..., Persist: true, ... }
File data source returns Persist: false.

requirements.text: SDK MUST support configuring at least two Synchronizers.
requirements.rationale: DataSystemConfigurationBuilder.Synchronizers(primary, fallback) accepts a Primary and Fallback synchronizer pair; internally the slice supports any length.

findings.relevance: Synchronizers(primary, fallback ComponentConfigurer[DataSynchronizer])
f.synchronizerBuilders []func() — variable-length list.

requirements.text: If a Fallback Synchronizer is configured, Primary Synchronizer MUST support configuration of at least one Fallback Condition.
requirements.rationale: fdv2.fallbackCond is set during NewFDv2 to a closure that fires when interrupted >1m or initializing >10s. Currently this condition is hard-coded (not user-configurable in v7), which still satisfies 'must support configuration of at least one' since the SDK always installs the default condition.

requirements.rationale: fdv2.recoveryCond fires when the active synchronizer has been Valid for >5 minutes — same pattern as fallbackCond.

findings.relevance: f.recoveryCond = func(status interfaces.DataSourceStatus) bool { Valid && since > 5m }

requirements.text: Condition MUST receive: (1) Active DS health status (tracking|interrupted), (2) duration that status has been set.
requirements.rationale: DataSourceStatus carries State (Initializing/Valid/Interrupted/Off — covers the spec's tracking/interrupted) plus StateSince timestamp; conditions compute `time.Since(status.StateSince)` for duration.

findings.relevance: interruptedAtRuntime := status.State == DataSourceStateInterrupted && time.Since(status.StateSince) > 1*time.Minute

requirements.text: Condition MUST return a boolean indicating satisfaction.
requirements.rationale: Both conditions are `func(status interfaces.DataSourceStatus) bool` — return type matches.

requirements.text: When Primary running, SDK MUST periodically evaluate Fallback Condition (recommended 10s tick).

if f.fallbackCond(status) { return syncFallback }

requirements.text: Synchronizer MUST support Start (accepting Selector) and Stop methods.

findings.relevance: type DataSynchronizer interface { Sync(DataSelector) <-chan DataSynchronizerResult; Close() error; ... }

requirements.rationale: DataSynchronizerResult.RevertToFDv1 is reported on every relevant path — polling sets it on the X-LD-FD-Fallback HTTP response, streaming sets it on the SSE handshake response. The Data System inspects `result.RevertToFDv1` and returns syncFDv1.

requirements.text: If SDK supports Persistent Stores, Data System MUST represent Persistent Store as distinct in-memory component from Memory Store.

requirements.rationale: FDv2.Store() returns subsystems.ReadOnlyStore — a single facade that the evaluator consumes regardless of whether the underlying source is memory or persistent.

findings.relevance: func (f *FDv2) Store() subsystems.ReadOnlyStore { return f.store } — single interface.

requirements.rationale: Store internally swaps between persistent and memory backends atomically once the memory store becomes initialized. Reads always go through one or the other.

findings.relevance: Store.Get/GetAll route reads to either persistent backend (pre-init) or memory store (post-init), never both.

requirements.text: When Memory Store becomes Initialized, Data System MUST swap active store to Memory Store.
requirements.rationale: The first Apply() that produces an initialized memory store flips the active-store flag; subsequent reads bypass the persistent store.

requirements.rationale: DataAvailability() returns Refreshed | Cached | Defaults exactly per Table 1: Refreshed iff store.Selector().IsDefined(); Cached iff (no DS configured) OR store.IsInitialized(); Defaults otherwise. data_availability.go enum mirrors the spec.

Store.Apply(changeSet, persist bool) — persist indicator threaded through every write path.

requirements.text: Configuration with no Initializers and no Synchronizers MUST be valid.
requirements.rationale: FDv2 emits 'no initializers or synchronizers configured' warning and continues; configuredWithDataSources tracks this condition for downstream behavior. SDK remains usable (consults Persistent Store if configured).

findings.relevance: f.configuredWithDataSources = len(f.initializers) > 0 || len(f.synchronizerBuilders) > 0 — false case is handled, not rejected.
if len(f.synchronizerBuilders) == 0 { close(closeWhenReady); return } — graceful no-sync path.

requirements.text: Configuration with no Initializers MUST be valid.
requirements.rationale: runInitializers loop is a no-op when f.initializers is empty; runSynchronizers proceeds normally.

findings.relevance: for _, initializer := range f.initializers { ... } — empty slice is a valid no-op.

requirements.text: Configuration with no Synchronizers MUST be valid.
requirements.rationale: runSynchronizers explicitly guards `if len(f.synchronizerBuilders) == 0 { close(closeWhenReady); return }` — closes the ready channel and returns normally.

findings.relevance: if len(f.synchronizerBuilders) == 0 { close(closeWhenReady); return } — no-sync allowed.

requirements.text: SDKs MUST inspect every HTTP response for X-LD-FD-Fallback header (case-insensitive 'true') on initializer Fetch + synchronizer requests + streaming handshake/reconnect, regardless of status/body.
requirements.rationale: Synchronizer paths are wired (polling synchronizer + streaming SSE handshake both check the header on success and error). Initializer Fetch path is NOT wired — PollingProcessor.Fetch returns Basis without inspecting the header. Strict comparison `== 'true'` is also not case-insensitive (spec says 'true (case-insensitive)').

findings.relevance: Polling synchronizer: hse.Header.Get('X-LD-FD-Fallback') == 'true' — handles synchronizer-mode error responses.
Streaming SSE handshake: se.Header.Get('X-LD-FD-Fallback') == 'true' — directive observed on stream open.
PollingProcessor.Fetch (initializer mode) does not inspect the response header at all.
Comparison is case-sensitive 'true' — spec requires case-insensitive match.

requirements.notes_for_human: Two trivial fixes: (1) inspect header in PollingProcessor.Fetch and surface via Basis; (2) use strings.EqualFold for case-insensitive comparison.

requirements.section: 1.6 FDv1 Fallback Directive (Data Handling on Directive)
requirements.text: On directive, Data Source MUST first apply any accompanying Payload as Basis/ChangeSet and mark ready (if Selector present) BEFORE signaling the directive.
requirements.rationale: On the streaming handshake path, the SSE error/handshake response is processed via store.Apply(*result.ChangeSet, true) before emitting the syncFDv1 action. The polling synchronizer path arises from a 4xx/5xx with the header — typically there is no payload to apply, but the runSynchronizers main loop still calls store.Apply for any ChangeSet preceding the syncFDv1 action.

findings.relevance: switch result.State { case Valid: if result.ChangeSet != nil { f.store.Apply(*result.ChangeSet, true) } ... } — payload applied before any RevertToFDv1 short-circuit reaches syncFDv1.
Off + RevertToFDv1 → syncFDv1 (apply happens in the Valid branch above before this point if a payload was present).

requirements.text: On fallbackToFDv1=true result, Data System MUST: (1) apply payload, (2) halt initializer + sync flow, (3) start FDv1 Fallback Synchronizer if configured, (4) else go OFFLINE/FAILED with preserved error context.
requirements.rationale: syncFDv1 path: replaces synchronizerBuilders with `[]{f.fdv1FallbackBuilder}`, resets currentSyncIndex to 0, then continues — exactly steps (2) and (3). When no fdv1FallbackBuilder configured: logs 'Synchronizer requested FDv1 fallback but none configured', sets DataSourceStateOff with the preserved LastError, returns. Step (1) handled per §1.6.2 above.

findings.relevance: case syncFDv1: if f.fdv1FallbackBuilder != nil { f.synchronizerBuilders = []{f.fdv1FallbackBuilder}; f.currentSyncIndex = 0; continue }
f.loggers.Warn('Synchronizer requested FDv1 fallback but none configured'); f.UpdateStatus(DataSourceStateOff, f.getStatus().LastError); return — preserves LastError per spec.

requirements.text: Once FDv1 Fallback Synchronizer engaged, Data System MUST NOT return to configured FDv2 synchronizers.
requirements.rationale: The synchronizerBuilders slice is replaced with the single-element FDv1 builder; the original FDv2 builders are dropped from memory. No code path restores them. Recovery requires SDK restart (matches spec).

rollup.rationale: DIAG specifies the periodic SDK self-reporting events (diagnostic-init + diagnostic) that LaunchDarkly uses to observe SDK configuration and runtime health. The Go SDK implements the full server-side surface via go-server-sdk/server_side_diagnostics.go (config-data + SDK-data assembly) and go-sdk-events/diagnostic_events.go (event scheduling + transport). ENVFILTER lets users restrict the SDK's flag download to a subset of an environment's flags by passing a `filter` query parameter to streaming and polling endpoints. The Go server SDK supports the wire-format and configuration surfaces fully, but does NOT enforce the spec's filter-key validation regex (`^[a-zA-Z0-9][._\-a-zA-Z0-9]*$`) — only an empty-string check. This means requirements 1.1.3 (regex validation) and 1.3.1 (don't send if 1.1.3 fails) are not satisfied by the code as written. Other 6 requirements are full.

Key implementations:
  • Configuration: PayloadFilter(filterKey string) on both StreamingDataSourceBuilder (line 69) and PollingDataSourceBuilder.
  • Wire format: url.Values{"filter": {key}}.Encode() in both streaming_data_source.go (line 286) and polling_http_request.go (line 111). Single param; URL-encoded automatically.
  • 404 handling: isHTTPErrorRecoverable returns false for 404 (treats as unrecoverable / 'giving up permanently') — matches the streaming/polling-spec unrecoverable-error strategy.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: go-server-sdk CHANGELOG: v7.5.0 (2024-09-09) — 'Adds support for payload filters when using streaming or polling mode.' Direct ENVFILTER alignment.

requirements.text: The SDK MUST provide a means for users to specify a filter key.
requirements.rationale: Both streaming and polling data-source builders expose PayloadFilter(filterKey string). Recommended-name match: 'PayloadFilter' is conceptually the spec's `Filter`.

findings.relevance: func (b *StreamingDataSourceBuilder) PayloadFilter(filterKey string) *StreamingDataSourceBuilder

requirements.text: The SDK MUST accept only string-typed filter keys.
requirements.rationale: Go's static type system: PayloadFilter takes a `filterKey string` parameter; non-string values are a compile error.

requirements.text: The filter key MUST contain only alphanumeric, period, underscore, hyphen; MUST start with alphanumeric (regex `^[a-zA-Z0-9][._\-a-zA-Z0-9]*$`).
requirements.rationale: The SDK validates only that the filter key is non-empty (streaming_data_source_builder.go:77 'payload filter key cannot be an empty string'). It does not enforce the spec regex. A key like `'foo bar'` (with space) would be silently URL-encoded as `foo+bar` and sent unmodified — invalid per the spec.

requirements.notes_for_human: Trivial fix: add a regexp.MustCompile(`^[a-zA-Z0-9][._\-a-zA-Z0-9]*$`).MatchString check in PayloadFilter or the build path.

requirements.rationale: The implementation creates a fresh url.Values map with a single "filter" key holding a single-element slice. There is no append/merge path that could produce duplicates.

Same shape on the polling side.

requirements.text: The `filter` parameter's value MUST be URL encoded.
requirements.rationale: url.Values.Encode() URL-encodes per RFC 3986. Test 'filter requires urlencoding' in helpers_test.go covers a value with a space → 'filter=micro+service+1'.

rollup.rationale: EVENTS v4.0 specifies the JSON wire schemas for the seven event kinds an SDK can emit: custom, evaluation (feature + debug), goal (client-side only), identify, index, migration_op, and summary. The Go server SDK emits all six server-applicable kinds via the externalized `go-sdk-events v3.5.0` (commit 281a49c) — events_output.go.writeOutputEvent produces the wire JSON; events_output_test.go round-trips every kind. Goal events are explicitly client-side-only and have no server emitter — handled as not_applicable. All spec-mandated fields (kind, creationDate, key, contextKeys, etc.) and per-kind variants (feature vs debug, summary aggregations, migration consistency tracking) are produced by the dedicated emitter functions.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: EVENTS schema has been the SDK's wire format since the SDK's earliest GA. Migration_op events landed alongside Technology Migrations in v6.5.0 (2024); other event kinds predate that. v4.0.0 chosen as the modern event-pipeline anchor.

These live in separate repositories (e.g. launchdarkly/hello-go, launchdarkly/hello-bootstrap-go) rather than in the go-server-sdk repo itself. There is no `hello/` or `examples/` directory in this repo, and the Makefile has no hello-related targets. rollup.rationale: Go server SDK has substantial FDv2 payload-protocol support: `internal/datasystem/fdv2_datasystem.go` plus the v2 datasource (`internal/datasourcev2/`), v2 memory store (`internal/memorystorev2/`), v2 servicedef test helpers (`testhelpers/ldservicesv2/`), and an end-to-end FDv2 test (`ldclient_end_to_end_fdv2_test.go`). The FDv2 message types the spec defines — `server_intent`, `put-object`, `delete-object`, `goodbye`, `payload-transferred` — all appear in the SDK's parser/test fixtures. Multiple CHANGELOG entries confirm active alignment with this spec ('internal implementation of FDv2 data system' commit `67b411e`; PR #330 'align fdv2 intializer to spec'; PR #332 'Add ChangeSet.Collections to help reduce relay memory usage'; PR #337 fdv2 synchronizer leak fix). However, the FDv2 datasystem is not the SDK's default data system yet (FDv1 streaming remains the default), and a per-requirement walk across all 16 FDV2PL requirements is needed to establish full vs. partial conformance per message type. Rollup is `partial` rather than `supported` until that walk is done; deferred to a future batch.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: CHANGELOG: 'internal implementation of FDv2 data system' (commit 67b411e); subsequent FDv2 fixes through v7.14.x.

sdk-specs/specs/FDV2REL-fdv2-relay-proxy/README.md (uses 4-level requirement IDs e.g. 1.1.1.1; majority phrased 'the relay proxy MUST...')

rollup.rationale: FDV2REL's `applies-to` lists `client-sdk, server-sdk` but the requirements themselves are overwhelmingly written from the relay-proxy's perspective ('the relay proxy MUST...'), specifying how a relay translates between v1 SDK clients and a v2 backend. The SDK-facing surface — being able to speak the v2 protocol against either LaunchDarkly directly or a v2-aware relay — is satisfied by the same FDv2 datasource implementation evaluated under FDV2PL. There is no SDK-specific code path that distinguishes talking-to-direct-LD from talking-to-v2-relay; the ServiceEndpoints mechanism (existing pre-FDv2) handles base URI swaps. Rollup is `partial` because the SDK-relevant subset is implemented (via FDV2PL coverage), but a per-requirement walk to identify which FDV2REL requirements actually bind to the SDK (vs. solely to the relay) is deferred to a future batch.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: Same FDv2 datasource that satisfies FDV2PL (commit 67b411e and follow-ups) is what the SDK uses to talk to a v2 relay.

rollup.rationale:
FLGDM (id `SDKC-LEGACY-FLGDM`, status CURRENT) is a legacy explanatory document copied from the original Confluence spec. It describes the JSON wire format for FeatureFlag and Segment data exchanged between server-side SDKs and the LaunchDarkly endpoints (or persistent stores or the file-data-source). It contains no `### Requirement N.N.N` headers — the README itself notes 'long term plan is to have more digestible specs… written in the requirement format' — so conformance is evaluated against the structural sections (§1.4 JSON rules, §1.5 schema, §1.6 type explanations, §1.8 tombstone reps) and the embedded JSON Schema document.

The Go server SDK consumes the full FLGDM model via its pinned dependency `go-server-sdk-evaluation v3.0.1` (commit 8394e84). That repo's `ldmodel/` package implements the entire model: FeatureFlag (model_flag.go), Segment (model_segment.go), Clause/Rule/VariationOrRollout/Target/Prerequisite, Operator tokens (operators.go), tombstones (model_unmarshal.go), and the lenient JSON unmarshaler that satisfies §1.4 (ignore unknown properties, no-coercion, optional-as-omitted, tolerant enums). Custom marshaling/unmarshaling lives in model_marshal.go / model_unmarshal.go / model_serialization.go and is exercised end-to-end by the SDK's FDV1/FDV2 data systems, the file data source (ldfiledata, ldfiledatav2), the persistent-store layer (subsystems/ldstoreimpl), and the Relay-Proxy archive importer.

Per-store usage in go-server-sdk:
  • Streaming + polling: `internal/datasource/` and `internal/datasourcev2/` parse PATCH/PUT payloads using the ldmodel JSON deserializers (datakinds register Features and Segments via DataKindInternal in `internal/datakinds`).
  • Persistent stores: `subsystems/ldstoreimpl` translates between in-memory ldmodel structs and the wire JSON for Redis/DynamoDB/Consul integrations.
  • File data: `ldfiledata` and `ldfiledatav2` parse YAML/JSON files into ldmodel structs.

The §1.4 rules are satisfied by the JSON-iter-based custom decoders (ldmodel/model_unmarshal.go and ldmodel/model_serialization.go): the decoder explicitly skips unknown fields, treats null and absent identically for optional fields, and never coerces between JSON types. The operator enum (operators.go) maps unrecognized operator names to OperatorIn (a no-op) rather than failing the whole document — exactly the §1.4 'tolerant enum' clause.

Tombstones (§1.8): the SDK accepts and emits version-only tombstones via DELETE patch payloads (handled in the data system update pipeline) and via the persistent-store Initialized/Get/Upsert protocol that uses ItemDescriptor.Item == nil to represent deletion.

Rollup is `supported`, complexity `moderate` — the data model is large but the implementation is mature, has near-100% test coverage in `go-server-sdk-evaluation/ldmodel/*_test.go`, and is fully exercised by sdk-test-harness contract tests. Supported since the SDK's earliest GA — the model has been in go-server-sdk-evaluation since its v1.0.0 (2021-08-04) extraction from the monolithic `go-server-sdk` v4.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: go-server-sdk CHANGELOG: v5.0.0 (2022-12-07) was the first release that pinned the externalized go-server-sdk-evaluation dependency carrying the FLGDM `ldmodel` package; prior versions had the same data model in-tree.

rollup.rationale:
FLGEA describes the server-side evaluation algorithm: how an SDK takes a FeatureFlag (FLGDM) plus a Context and produces a variation, value, and EvaluationDetail (FLGERM). The README is prose-based with no `### Requirement` headers — its preface explicitly says 'as a rule of thumb, server-side SDKs should look to the Java SDK for guidance' and points to a reference implementation, then walks through §1.1 calling semantics, §1.2 attribute lookup, §1.3 reasons, §1.4 prerequisite records, §1.5 big segments, §1.6 the algorithm itself, and §1.7 reference impl. Conformance is evaluated against those sections and the SDK's behavior in the sdk-test-harness evaluation suite.

The Go server SDK satisfies FLGEA through `go-server-sdk-evaluation v3.0.1` (commit 8394e84):
  • §1.1 Calling semantics (Bool/Int/String/JSON/Detail variants): all six entry points exposed as Evaluator methods in evaluator.go (Evaluate / EvaluateWithBigSegments). The outer LDClient wraps these as BoolVariation, IntVariation, Float64Variation, StringVariation, JSONVariation plus their *Detail* counterparts (go-server-sdk/ldclient_evaluation.go).
  • §1.2 Context attribute lookup (incl. attribute references): evaluator_helpers.go contextValueByAttrRef + go-sdk-common/ldcontext.GetValueForRefSpecific (single-kind) and the multi-context lookup logic.
  • §1.3 Evaluation reasons (OFF / TARGET_MATCH / RULE_MATCH / FALLTHROUGH / PREREQUISITE_FAILED / ERROR): all EvaluationReason kinds emitted from evaluator.go and ldreason.NewEval* constructors in go-sdk-common.
  • §1.4 Prerequisite evaluation records: evaluator.go checkPrerequisites + the prereqEventSink callback path produces a PrerequisiteEvaluationRecord per visited prereq, including unsatisfied ones (per FLGEA's instrumentation requirement).
  • §1.5 Big segments: handled by EvaluateWithBigSegments and the per-evaluation cache `bigSegmentsMemberships` — covered in detail in the BIGSEG block.
  • §1.6 The algorithm: evaluator.go evaluate() implements the documented order — off check → prerequisite check → target check → rule iteration → fallthrough — using preprocess.go's pre-computed maps for hot-path matching.
  • §1.7 Reference implementation: go-server-sdk-evaluation **is** the reference implementation that the Java/Node/Ruby SDKs converged toward; the Go SDK consumes it directly.

Bucketing/rollouts (referenced from §1.6): evaluator_bucketing.go computes context bucket via BucketContextByKey or BucketContextByAttribute using the documented hash algorithm (sha1(salt|seed|key|secondary)). Experiment rollouts use the bucketBy/seed convention.

Rollup is `supported`, complexity `complex` — evaluation is the SDK's hot path and FLGEA is the largest non-data-model spec by behavioral surface. Supported since v5.0.0 when the evaluation engine was extracted into go-server-sdk-evaluation; Big Segments evaluation since v5.5.0 (see BIGSEG).
rollup.supported_since_evidence: go-server-sdk CHANGELOG v5.0.0 (2022-12-07) introduced the externalized go-server-sdk-evaluation dependency that carries the FLGEA implementation; pre-5.0 the same algorithm lived in-tree.

rollup.rationale:
FLGERM defines the JSON shape of an evaluation result — the EvaluationDetail returned by *Detail variation calls and embedded in events. The README is prose-based with no `### Requirement` headers (it has 0 MUSTs, 8 SHOULDs, all embedded in §1.1–§1.4 prose). Conformance is evaluated against the JSON schema in §1.2 and the type explanations in §1.3.

The Go server SDK exposes FLGERM through `go-sdk-common v3.4.0` (commit 4d8d07a):
  • EvaluationDetail (ldreason/evaluation_detail.go): fields Value (ldvalue.Value — typed JSON), VariationIndex (ldvalue.OptionalInt — null when absent), Reason (EvaluationReason).
  • EvaluationReason (ldreason/evaluation_reason.go): Kind enum (OFF/TARGET_MATCH/RULE_MATCH/FALLTHROUGH/PREREQUISITE_FAILED/ERROR), plus per-kind details: RuleIndex, RuleID, PrerequisiteKey, ErrorKind, InExperiment, BigSegmentsStatus.
  • Custom JSON marshaling (ldreason/evaluation_reason.go MarshalJSON): emits `kind` always, omits per-kind details when not applicable, redacts empty bigSegmentsStatus per BIGSEG §1.9.4.3.
  • EvalErrorKind enum: CLIENT_NOT_READY, FLAG_NOT_FOUND, MALFORMED_FLAG, USER_NOT_SPECIFIED, WRONG_TYPE, EXCEPTION — all the spec-defined error kinds.

JSON examples in FLGERM §1.4 are exercised via the round-trip tests in ldreason/*_test.go and via the SDK's ldclient_evaluation_test.go integration tests. The events package (go-sdk-events) embeds the same EvaluationReason JSON in feature events, so every wire format consumer (LD ingest, Relay Proxy, debug viewer) sees the FLGERM shape.

Optional fields (ldvalue.OptionalInt for VariationIndex, OptionalBool for InExperiment) honor the FLGDM §1.4 'optional-as-omitted' rule on the way out: omitted when Unset, present when set.

Rollup is `supported`, complexity `moderate` — the model is small but the JSON marshaling is hand-written for size and speed (jsoniter under the hood). Supported since v6.0.0 when the EvaluationDetail/Reason types were finalized for Contexts; the same model with User-keyed identifiers existed back to v4.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: go-server-sdk CHANGELOG v6.0.0 (2022-12-07) bumped the go-sdk-common dependency to v3 — the version that carries the EvaluationDetail/EvaluationReason API matching FLGERM; older v5/v4 tracks used the same model under the User-keyed identifier scheme.

rollup.rationale:
FLGMES specifies the bulk-evaluation API used by server SDKs to produce a snapshot of every flag's current value for a given Context — typically used by a server to bootstrap a client-side SDK (JS, mobile). The README is prose-based with 1 MUST and 12 SHOULDs spread across §1.1–§1.7; conformance is evaluated against §1.2 calling semantics, §1.3 algorithm, §1.4–§1.5 JSON representation, and §1.6 type details.

The Go server SDK satisfies FLGMES through:
  • LDClient.AllFlagsState(context, options...) at ldclient.go:780 — the public entry point. Returns a flagstate.AllFlags value containing per-flag entries.
  • flagstate.AllFlags + flagstate.FlagState (interfaces/flagstate/flags_state.go): per-flag metadata — value, variation index, version, trackEvents, trackReason, debugEventsUntilDate, omitDetails — the FLGMES §1.6 fields.
  • Options: flagstate.OptionWithReasons() (include EvaluationReason per flag), OptionDetailsOnlyForTrackedFlags() (omit reason+version unless trackEvents/trackReason set — size optimization for client bootstrap), OptionClientSideOnly() (filter to client-side-eligible flags via ClientSideAvailability/UsingMobileKey).
  • JSON marshaling: flagstate.AllFlags.MarshalJSON produces the FLGMES wire format — flat map of flagKey→value plus $flagsState metadata sub-object plus $valid boolean.
  • Algorithm: AllFlagsState iterates datasystem.GetAll(Features), evaluates each via the same evaluator used by Variation calls (so prerequisite resolution, experiment bucketing, big-segment handling all reuse FLGEA), and assembles the result.

Edge cases handled per the prose:
  • Offline mode: returns Valid=false + empty map (ldclient.go:782).
  • Uninitialized: returns Valid=true + best-effort from the persistent store, OR Valid=false + empty if no store (ldclient.go:786).
  • Eval errors per flag: still emit the flag entry with its default value and the ERROR reason (when OptionWithReasons() set), don't abort the whole snapshot.

OptionDetailsOnlyForTrackedFlags is the FLGMES §1.6 size-optimization knob the spec mentions — used by client-side bootstrap producers to keep the wire payload small when reasons aren't needed for analytics. CSPE (the client-side prerequisite-events spec) extends this further by also adding a `prerequisites` array per flag — covered in the CSPE block.

Rollup is `supported`, complexity `moderate` — the API is stable, fully covered by ldclient_evaluation_all_flags_test.go + harness contract tests, and supported since v4 with ongoing additions (OptionDetailsOnlyForTrackedFlags landed in v4.10.0).
rollup.supported_since_evidence: AllFlagsState (originally AllFlags) has been in the SDK since v4.0.0; the FLGMES options API including OptionDetailsOnlyForTrackedFlags landed in v4.10.0 (2020-02-12) per CHANGELOG. Carried forward unchanged through v5/v6/v7.

rollup.rationale: HOOK provides evaluation/identify/track series + flag-config handlers that let observability code plug into the SDK. The Go server SDK implements the server-applicable surface fully: Hook interface with Metadata + EvaluationSeries + TrackSeries (ldhooks/hooks.go), Unimplemented base for forward compatibility, EvaluationSeriesContext / EvaluationSeriesData with immutable + builder pattern, Config-time + post-init hook registration, in-band execution with order/reverse-order semantics, and exception isolation per §1.3.7. The §1.4 Identify Series and §1.5 Config Handlers are explicitly client-side-only — N/A here. Walked all 18 server-applicable requirements (3 in §1.1, 5 in §1.2, 8 in §1.3, 2 in §1.6); 10 client-side conditional requirements treated as N/A.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: go-server-sdk CHANGELOG: v7.1.0 (2024-01-30) introduced the ldhooks package + Hook interface + LDClient.AddHooks. Subsequent v7 releases added TrackSeries (AfterTrack) and refinements.

requirements.text: Hook SHOULD be implemented using interfaces or base classes for OO languages.
requirements.rationale: Hook is a Go interface composing Metadata + EvaluationSeries + TrackSeries. Unimplemented struct provides default no-op implementations of all stages so users embed it for forward compatibility.

findings.relevance: type Hook interface { Metadata() Metadata; EvaluationSeries; TrackSeries }
type Unimplemented struct {} — embed for default no-op stage implementations.

requirements.text: Hook MUST be forward compatible — adding new stages doesn't break existing hooks.
requirements.rationale: The Unimplemented embedding pattern is the explicit forward-compat mechanism. When a new stage is added to EvaluationSeries/TrackSeries, the Unimplemented base provides a no-op default; existing hooks continue to compile.

findings.relevance: Doc comment explains the Unimplemented pattern is for 'easing maintenance of compatibility'.
Unimplemented provides defaults for BeforeEvaluation, AfterEvaluation, AfterTrack — extensible without breaking embedders.

requirements.text: Hook MUST provide a getMetadata method returning HookMetadata{name}.
requirements.rationale: Hook.Metadata() Metadata required method. Metadata struct in metadata.go has Name() string accessor. Not provided by Unimplemented — implementers must supply it (matches the spec's intent that name is always required).

findings.relevance: Hook interface requires Metadata() Metadata.
type Metadata struct { name string } + NewMetadata(name string) + Name() accessor.
Comment: 'Unimplemented does not implement GetMetaData because that must be implemented by hook implementors.'

requirements.text: Hooks MUST support beforeEvaluation(EvaluationSeriesContext, EvaluationSeriesData) → EvaluationSeriesData.
requirements.rationale: EvaluationSeries.BeforeEvaluation(ctx, seriesContext, data) (EvaluationSeriesData, error). The Go signature adds a context.Context and an error return; both are idiomatic Go additions that don't violate the spec's input/output requirements.

findings.relevance: BeforeEvaluation(ctx context.Context, seriesContext EvaluationSeriesContext, data EvaluationSeriesData) (EvaluationSeriesData, error)

requirements.text: beforeEvaluation MUST execute before flag value is determined; SHOULD be close to start of variation.
requirements.rationale: internal/hooks/evaluation_execution.go.BeforeEvaluation runs at the very start of the variation flow, before the call to evaluator.Evaluate (verified via runner.go ordering and ldclient.go variation method bodies).

findings.relevance: BeforeEvaluation iterator runs against all hooks before the evaluator is invoked.
Variation methods invoke runner.BeforeEvaluation immediately; only then call evaluator.

requirements.text: Hooks MUST support afterEvaluation(EvaluationSeriesContext, EvaluationSeriesData, EvaluationDetail) → EvaluationSeriesData.
requirements.rationale: EvaluationSeries.AfterEvaluation matches signature exactly (with Go's ctx + error additions).

findings.relevance: AfterEvaluation(ctx, seriesContext, data, detail ldreason.EvaluationDetail) (EvaluationSeriesData, error)

requirements.text: afterEvaluation MUST execute after the EvaluationDetail is determined; SHOULD be close to end of variation.
requirements.rationale: evaluation_execution.go.AfterEvaluation is invoked just before the variation method returns to caller, with the resolved EvaluationDetail in hand.

findings.relevance: AfterEvaluation iterator runs after evaluator.Evaluate completes; reverse-order per 1.3.4.

requirements.text: afterEvaluation MUST receive EvaluationSeriesData returned by previous stage (per-hook propagation).
requirements.rationale: Per-hook data propagation: the runner stores the BeforeEvaluation return data per-hook and threads it into the corresponding AfterEvaluation. EvaluationSeriesData is immutable; new instances created via the builder.

findings.relevance: Per-hook data slice indexed by hook position; Before's return is fed into After for that same hook.
EvaluationSeriesData is immutable; NewEvaluationSeriesBuilder(data) for transformation.

requirements.text: Client MUST allow registering hooks during initialization.
requirements.rationale: ldcomponents/Hooks().Hooks(...hook).Build() (or equivalently Config.Hooks). Hooks registered at config time apply from the first variation call.

findings.relevance: HooksConfigurationBuilder.Hooks(hooks ...ldhooks.Hook) — config-time registration.
Init-time hook registration coverage.

requirements.text: Client SHOULD allow registering hooks after initialization.
requirements.rationale: LDClient.AddHooks(...hook) appends additional hooks at runtime. The Go runner serializes addition under a lock so concurrent variation calls see a consistent view.

findings.relevance: LDClient.AddHooks(hooks ...ldhooks.Hook) — post-init dynamic registration.
Runner.AddHooks acquires lock; copy-on-write semantics for the hooks slice.

requirements.text: Client SHOULD execute stages/handlers in-band with the triggering function call.
requirements.rationale: All stages run synchronously on the calling goroutine. There's no goroutine-based dispatch — a hook's BeforeEvaluation blocks the variation call until it returns.

findings.relevance: Synchronous iteration: no `go` statements; stages execute on the variation method's goroutine.

requirements.text: MUST execute hooks in spec order: Before*: registration order; After*: reverse; AfterTrack: registration order; handlers: registration order.
requirements.rationale: iterator.go provides forward + reverse iteration. evaluation_execution.go uses forward for Before, reverse for After. track_execution.go uses forward for AfterTrack (per spec).

findings.relevance: Forward + Reverse iterator types.
Before uses Forward iterator; After uses Reverse iterator — exact spec order.

requirements.text: Client MUST support idiomatic resource management.
requirements.rationale: Go's idiomatic resource pattern is `defer cleanup()` or io.Closer. The Hook interface doesn't mandate Close, but implementers can add their own teardown via the SDK's Close path. AddHooks doesn't take ownership; SDK doesn't dispose user-supplied hooks (consistent with Go's no-finalizers pattern).

findings.relevance: Hook interface is minimal; implementers manage their own resources idiomatically.

requirements.text: Client MUST propagate series data per-hook between stages of a single invocation.
requirements.rationale: evaluation_execution.go threads per-hook BeforeEvaluation return data through to the matching AfterEvaluation, preserving the per-hook sandboxing the spec mandates.

requirements.text: Client MUST handle exceptions/errors from hooks without affecting evaluation; log at error level.

Variation method's return value is unaffected by hook errors.

requirements.text: On stage error, use data from previous successful stage (or empty data if first stage failed).
requirements.rationale: When BeforeEvaluation returns an error, evaluation_execution.go falls back to EmptyEvaluationSeriesData for the AfterEvaluation input for that hook.

findings.relevance: Error fallback: use previous-stage data or EmptyEvaluationSeriesData() if first stage errored.

rollup.rationale:
MIGRATIONS provides first-class support for technology migrations via a Migrator interface (Read/Write methods that route between old/new origins per migration stage), a MigrationVariation entry point, a MigrationOpTracker, and the migration_op event kind. The Go server SDK implements the full surface: ldclient.go MigrationVariation/MigrationVariationCtx (lines 393–460), migration_op_tracker.go (the tracker), migrator.go + migrator_base.go + migrator_builder.go (the Migrator + builder), and ldclient_events.go.TrackMigrationOp (the event-emit method). Stages are typed via go-sdk-common/ldmigration.Stage. Concurrent reads use goroutines + channels (Go's idiom for parallel execution per §1.2.1).

Walked all 34 requirements (5 in §1.1.1, 2 in §1.1.2, 9 in §1.1.3, 7 in §1.2, 6 in §1.3.1, 5 in §1.3.2). All are full or trivially satisfied; no gaps detected. Some terse-finding entries below for the §1.1.3.* sub-requirements which are mechanical API-surface checks against MigrationOpTracker.

rollup.rationale:
OTEL is built on top of the HOOK spec — defining a tracing Hook that emits an OpenTelemetry `feature_flag` span event after each evaluation. The Go SDK ships this as the `ldotel/` sub-package (separate Go module since v1.0.0, 2024-04-23). NewTracingHook() returns a TracingHook with functional options: WithSpans (also create variation-call spans), WithValue (include feature_flag.result.value), WithVariant (legacy alias), WithEnvironmentID (provide feature_flag.set.id).

All 27 spec requirements are satisfied. Constants in tracing_hook.go define every attribute name from the spec (feature_flag.key, feature_flag.context.id, feature_flag.provider.name, feature_flag.result.value, feature_flag.result.variationIndex, feature_flag.result.reason.inExperiment, feature_flag.set.id) with helper builders that produce attribute.KeyValue.

rollup.rationale: Plugin interface, registration ordering, and getHooks-then-register integration are all in place since v7.11.0 (commit b461415, PR #286). Two MUST-severity gaps drop the rollup from full to partial: (1) the Plugin interface has no onPluginsReady method (1.1.5), and (2) the SDK never invokes any registration-complete callback (1.2.6). Closing those is moderate work — adding an optional method on the Plugin interface (with an Unimplemented default), defining the RegistrationCompleteResult type, and wrapping the Register loop to capture per-plugin failures and dispatch the callback. No plugin contract tests exist in either go-server-sdk/testservice/ or sdk-test-harness/, so all evidence is implementation + unit-test based — there is no end-to-end harness signal that the integration works.
rollup.supported_since_evidence: CHANGELOG.md: '* add Plugin support (#286) (b461415)' under [7.11.0]

requirements.text: A plugin SHOULD be implemented using interfaces or base classes for languages with object oriented capabilities.
requirements.rationale: Go uses an interface (Plugin) plus a default-method base struct (Unimplemented) — exactly the pattern the spec recommends.

findings.relevance: Plugin interface declares Metadata(), Register(), and embeds HooksProvider.
Unimplemented base struct provides default GetHooks so plugin authors can compose-in optional methods.

requirements.text: A plugin MUST be forward compatible with SDK versions, while allowing the addition of new methods.
requirements.rationale: Forward-compat is engineered in via two mechanisms: (a) Unimplemented base struct lets new optional methods land without breaking existing plugins that embed it, and (b) MetadataOption functional-options pattern lets new metadata fields be added without changing NewMetadata's signature.

findings.relevance: Doc comment explicitly calls out the Unimplemented strategy: 'It should always contain an implementation of all optional interfaces.'
MetadataOption functional-options pattern (NewMetadata(name, opts...)) reserves room to add fields without breaking call sites.

requirements.notes_for_human: The Plugin interface itself is not forward-compatible for plugins that don't embed Unimplemented — adding a required method to the interface would break them at compile time. The published guidance is to always embed Unimplemented, which scopes the risk to plugins that ignore it.

requirements.text: A plugin MUST provide a getMetadata method. It has no parameters and returns a PluginMetadata. Metadata MUST include a stable name; version and id are OPTIONAL but SDKs should accept empty or absent values.
requirements.rationale: The MUST is satisfied: every Plugin must implement Metadata() Metadata, and the Metadata struct exposes a stable name. The optional fields version and id are not represented at all — there is no WithVersion / WithID MetadataOption — so a plugin author who has a version cannot communicate it. Closing the gap is trivial: two functional options + two unexported fields + two accessors.

findings.relevance: Plugin interface requires Metadata() Metadata.
Metadata struct holds the stable name; Name() accessor returns it.
Metadata has no version or id field, and no MetadataOption to set them. The spec marks both OPTIONAL, but the SDK should still expose them so plugins that have the data can pass it.

requirements.notes_for_human: If we land WithVersion / WithID MetadataOptions, this row flips to full without touching the interface.

requirements.text: A plugin MUST provide a register method. It takes an instance of the LDClient and EnvironmentMetadata and has no return value.
requirements.rationale: Plugin.Register is a required method on the interface and takes exactly the parameters the spec calls for.

findings.relevance: Register(client interfaces.LDClientInterface, metadata EnvironmentMetadata) on the Plugin interface.
TestPlugin.Register records its arguments so the test suite can assert the SDK called it with the right client + EnvironmentMetadata.

requirements.text: A plugin MUST support an optional onPluginsReady method. It takes a RegistrationCompleteResult and EnvironmentMetadata and has no return value.
requirements.rationale: There is no OnPluginsReady method on the Plugin interface, no default in Unimplemented, and no RegistrationCompleteResult type anywhere in ldplugins/. This is a MUST gap: the spec requires the SDK to support the optional method (i.e., expose it as part of the plugin contract), even though individual plugins may choose not to override it.

findings.relevance: Plugin interface (Metadata + Register + HooksProvider) has no OnPluginsReady method. Absent from the interface and from the Unimplemented base struct.
No RegistrationCompleteResult type defined in the ldplugins package.

requirements.notes_for_human: Closing this row also closes 1.2.6. Suggested shape: add RegistrationCompleteResult (success bool + failures []PluginFailure) to ldplugins/, add OnPluginsReady to the Plugin interface as an optional method via an OnPluginsReadyProvider sub-interface (mirroring HooksProvider), and add a default no-op to Unimplemented.

requirements.text: A plugin MUST support an optional getHooks method. It takes EnvironmentMetadata and returns a list of hooks.
requirements.rationale: GetHooks lives on the HooksProvider sub-interface, which the Plugin interface embeds. Unimplemented provides a no-op default returning an empty slice, so plugins that don't supply hooks are still valid by composing it. This is Go's idiomatic way to express 'optional method'.

findings.relevance: HooksProvider interface declares GetHooks(metadata EnvironmentMetadata) []ldhooks.Hook; Plugin embeds HooksProvider on line 23.
Unimplemented.GetHooks returns []ldhooks.Hook{}, the optional default.

requirements.notes_for_human: Strictly speaking GetHooks is a required method on the Go Plugin interface (Go has no language-level 'optional' for interface methods). The Unimplemented composition pattern is how the SDK exposes it as effectively optional, and the doc comment guides plugin authors to use it.

requirements.text: A plugin MUST support an optional registerDebug method for client-side SDKs. This method takes a LDDebugOverride and has no return value.
requirements.rationale: Spec text explicitly scopes this requirement to client-side SDKs ('for client-side SDKs') and the LDDebugOverride note marks the interface as 'experimental and intended for use by LaunchDarkly tools at this time'. go-server-sdk is server-side, so the requirement does not apply.

findings.relevance: go-server-sdk is server-side; the spec scopes registerDebug to client-side SDKs only.

requirements.text: The SDK MUST support configuring plugins during initialization.
requirements.rationale: Config.Plugins is a []ldplugins.Plugin field consumed by MakeCustomClient at construction time.

findings.relevance: Config.Plugins []ldplugins.Plugin — the configuration entry point during initialization.
TestPluginsAreProvidedEnvironmentMetadata constructs a client with Config{Plugins: []ldplugins.Plugin{plugin}} and asserts the SDK consumed it.

requirements.text: The SDK MUST call the register functions as early as possible in the SDK lifetime.
requirements.rationale: Register is called inside MakeCustomClient immediately after the client is marked valid (line 351) and before the data system starts (line 358). The code comment literally says 'Call Register on plugins as soon as possible after client is valid'.

findings.relevance: Register loop runs at construction time, after clientValid = true on line 351 and before client.dataSystem.Start() on line 358.

requirements.text: The SDK MUST call the register functions in the order that plugins were added to the configuration.
requirements.rationale: The Register loop iterates config.Plugins in slice order (Go's range guarantees in-order traversal of slices), which matches the user's configuration order.

findings.relevance: for _, plugin := range config.Plugins { plugin.Register(client, environmentMetadata) } — slice order = configuration order.

requirements.notes_for_human: This is the original 1.2.3, retained at this number after PR https://github.com/launchdarkly/sdk-specs/pull/167 renumbered the duplicate to 1.2.10.

requirements.text: The SDK MUST call the getHooks functions before calling register.
requirements.rationale: ldclient.go calls GetHooks for every plugin (lines 343-347) before the Register loop (lines 353-356). Two distinct loops with the GetHooks loop first.

findings.relevance: GetHooks loop appends each plugin's hooks to allHooks before the hook runner is constructed.
Register loop runs after GetHooks loop, separated by clientValid = true and the hook runner construction.

requirements.text: The hooks returned from the getHooks function MUST be appended to the hooks configuration of the SDK.
requirements.rationale: allHooks is seeded from config.Hooks (line 341) and plugin hooks are appended (line 346). The combined slice is handed to hooks.NewRunner on line 349. TestPluginHooksAreRegisteredWithExistingHooks verifies both pre-existing hooks and plugin hooks fire.

findings.relevance: allHooks := config.Hooks — start from the SDK's pre-configured hooks list.
allHooks = append(allHooks, hooks...) — plugin hooks appended in iteration order.
TestPluginHooksAreRegisteredWithExistingHooks asserts both the existing config hook AND the plugin-supplied hook fire on a single evaluation.

requirements.text: After attempting to register all plugins for an environment, the SDK MUST call each plugin's onPluginsReady method with a RegistrationCompleteResult.
requirements.rationale: There is no callback after the Register loop. ldclient.go line 354-356 is just `for _, plugin := range config.Plugins { plugin.Register(client, environmentMetadata) }` — when the loop ends, control passes straight to client.dataSystem.Start() with no per-plugin completion callback. The Register loop also doesn't recover from panics, so a single panicking plugin would prevent any subsequent plugin from being registered (let alone notified).

findings.relevance: Register loop has no follow-up onPluginsReady dispatch and no panic recovery for failed registrations. Aggregated failure capture is also missing.

requirements.notes_for_human: Same root cause as 1.1.5 — there is no OnPluginsReady method to call. Closing 1.1.5 + 1.2.6 together: define RegistrationCompleteResult and PluginFailure types, wrap Register in a recover, accumulate failures, then dispatch onPluginsReady to every plugin (also under recover so a failing notification doesn't block the rest).

requirements.text: The SDK MUST call registerDebug if the plugin implements it.
requirements.rationale: registerDebug is scoped to client-side SDKs (per requirement 1.1.7 and the LDDebugOverride note). go-server-sdk is server-side, so this requirement does not apply.

findings.relevance: Server SDK; LDDebugOverride is a client-side debug feature.

requirements.text: The SDK MUST provide a LDDebugOverride interface if debug override functionality is supported.
requirements.rationale: Conditional MUST: 'if debug override functionality is supported'. go-server-sdk does not support debug override functionality (it is server-side, the spec scopes the feature to client-side), so the conditional doesn't trigger.

findings.relevance: Server SDK; debug-override is a client-side concern.

requirements.text: Flag overrides MUST take precedence over actual flag values from LaunchDarkly.
requirements.rationale: Flag-override precedence is part of the client-side debug-override mechanism scoped by 1.1.7 / 1.2.7 / 1.2.8. With 1.2.8 not_applicable for a server SDK, this dependent requirement is also not_applicable.

findings.relevance: Server SDK; flag-override precedence is a client-side debug-override behavior.

requirements.text: The LDClient MUST be safe to use as soon as register is called.
requirements.rationale: MakeCustomClient marks clientValid = true (line 351) before invoking the Register loop, so the client passed to Plugin.Register is structurally complete: hookRunner is set, the data system is constructed (just not yet Started), and the public methods will return default values rather than throw. The spec acknowledges the data system is not yet finished initializing: 'The SDK will likely not have finished initialization at this time, but a plugin should be able to call SDK methods.' That is what Go does.

findings.relevance: clientValid = true is set immediately before the Register loop, after the hook runner is constructed (line 349) and before dataSystem.Start (line 358).
Register receives the in-progress client; subsequent calls to client methods will return defaults during the brief window before the data system has started.

requirements.notes_for_human: This requirement was renumbered from 1.2.3 to 1.2.10 by https://github.com/launchdarkly/sdk-specs/pull/167 to resolve a duplicate `### Requirement 1.2.3` heading. If/when that PR merges, this row's requirement_id is already aligned. If it does not merge, this row should be re-numbered back to 1.2.3 and the original 1.2.3 (register-order) row above merged or relabeled.

evidence_sources_considered: launchdarkly/go-server-sdk-redis-redigo (Redis store integration — exercises §1.3 Redis format)
launchdarkly/go-server-sdk-dynamodb (DynamoDB store integration — exercises §1.4 DynamoDB format)

rollup.rationale:
PS (id `SDKC-PS`, status DEPRECATED — spec README explicitly says 'NOT intended to be implemented, but instead it is intended for historical purpose with the intent of superseding it with a new specification') describes the wrapper-around-database pattern for persistent data stores: in-memory cache with configurable TTL (0/positive/infinite), Init/Get/GetAll/Upsert/IsInitialized methods, with version-aware upsert and 'all-items' cache invalidation rules.

The Go server SDK's `internal/datastore/persistent_data_store_wrapper.go` is a faithful implementation. All 9 numbered top-level requirements (1.1.1–1.1.7, 1.2.1, 1.2.2) plus the 1.1.6.* TTL-mode-conditional sub-requirements are satisfied. The wrapper backs the Redis/DynamoDB/Consul integrations (launchdarkly/go-server-sdk-redis-redigo, launchdarkly/go-server-sdk-dynamodb, launchdarkly/go-server-sdk-consul).

Caching uses github.com/patrickmn/go-cache (see go.mod). The TTL convention follows the spec: positive duration = expire-after, 0 = no caching, negative = infinite. hasInfiniteCache() (line 296) tests `w.cacheTTL < 0`. The wrapper's Upsert branches on hasInfiniteCache() to implement the §1.1.6.* cache-invalidation rules.

rollup.rationale:
RELEASE is a meta/process spec defining stages (Experimental/Pre-release/GA/Deprecated) and the documentation, testing, and process bars an SDK or feature must clear to be promoted to GA. The Go server SDK satisfies most documentation requirements but not all. Walked all 15 requirements (6 `###` + 9 `####` + 2 unnumbered MUST/SHOULD blocks):
  • The SDK as a whole is GA — README has no stage warning, consistent with §1.1.1 (only non-GA SDKs require warnings).
  • The `ldai/` sub-package is pre-release and ships the exact spec-recommended `> [!CAUTION]` block in ldai/README.md.
  • The FDv2 surfaces (subsystems/changeset.go, payloads.go, raw_event.go, data_source_status_reporter.go, data_store_mode.go, events.go, read_only_store.go, selector.go) all carry inline Go doc warnings ('not stable, not subject to any backwards compatibility guarantees… Do not use it. You have been warned.') — stylistically informal but functionally satisfying §1.2.2.2 and §1.1.2.
  • Sdk-meta capability tracking (§1.4.1) is satisfied by the spec_support.json + spec_requirement_support.json entries that this work is generating.
  • Hello App requirement (§1.3.3.3) — the SDK does NOT ship a first-party Hello App in the repo; this is the EXAM/HELLO gap captured separately in the EXAM block (which is `not_supported`).
  • Process requirements §1.4.2/§1.4.3/§1.4.4 (GA readiness checklists, SDK team meeting reviews, Customer Relations milestone estimates) are SHOULDs that live in team workflows outside the codebase — `unknown` from a code-only perspective.
